CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Happily the “ Hungarian ’’ Party have threc moder-
ately wealthy men in their ranks now who, we trust,
will make a good fight against the income tax. Even

Irish Unionists will, secretly at any rate, wish Sir .

Thomas Esmonde, Mr. Sweetman, and Mr. Martyn
well when they boldly refuse to pay that tax, and all
business men and manufacturers will watch the fight
of the noble;hearted three with an interest which they
seldom take in politics. We gather that one of the

- speakers at the meeting in the Pheenix Park attempted

to explain why Mr. Martyn and Mr. Sweetman had
up to this declined to heroically refuse to pay this tax
levied in Ireland, and we took the impression from the
* remarks as_reported that Mr. Swectman and Mr.

~ Martyn now being free to attack this tax would show

- fight on the next occasion. We hope they will. In
their. passive resistance against income tax they have

. _our hearty good wishes and support, and we think we

may say the same on behalf of the vast majority of our
readers who, we have no doubt, are victims of this tax,
and on behalf of our commercial and industrial adver-
sers. .One bold man can do a great deal, and three

“.«ﬁ)g be able to do three times as much. When Mr.

Swoetman, Mr. Martyn, and Sir T. Esmonde step into
the breach and refuse to pay this tax they may go down
to history as a trio of Hampdens. What matter if
they suflor some little inconvenience—after all they
loudly preach a policy of heroic sacrifice for Ireland,
and they will jump at an opportunity of practising it
—the industrial, commercial and professional people of
Ireland will bless their memories if they gain any re-
dress from the fight of the noble three.” We look for-
ward as public journalists and as private individuals to
this struggle. Men in business could not well afford
to take the risks of being pioneers in this struggle; but
three gentlemen of independent means appear to be cut
out by fate for what no doubt will be an epoch-making
fight on the overtaxation question of Ireland.

Enniscorthy is a complex place. Recently they had
a ‘“ Hungarian ’’ meeting there, and now we see that a
‘‘ Midsummer Entertainment > was held at St. Bene-
dict’s School in that town, which a little over a hundred
years ago was in the necighbourhood of fierce fighting,
and which this summer was the scene of resolutions,
eloquent orators and an ex-militiaman’s political con-

version. On Saturday last, at St. Benedict’s School,
a ‘ French Farce,”” entiled ‘“ La Grammaire,”’ was per-
formed. Whether the lads, who by the way included

a Master O. Esmonde, performed the farce in Ennis-
corthy French or in English we are in doubt. What
between Hungary and France and pulverising resolu-
tions, Enniscorthy may forget that it is adjacent to the
historic Vinegar Hill.

Is there not a rule that policemen or m jitiamen or
ex-policemen or ex-militiamen are not entitis1 to play
in G.A.A. matches! And yet, amid a volley of loud
and prolonged cheering, an ex-militiaman was welcomed
into Mr. Martyn’s ‘“ National Cour:12’! If Mr.
Martyn’s dictum about the flogging cf Tommies isretro-
spective, Mr. Martyn ought to be horsewhipping his
latest recruit, the ex-militiaman, Sir Thomas Esmonde!
Certainly the situation is mixed and piquant. Per-
haps, if Mr. Martyn took to flogging this ex-amafeur
Tommy, Sir Tommy might strike back!

Now that Sir T. Esmonde has become a ‘ Hun-
garian,’”’ we await his resignation as a director of the
so-called ““ National ”” Bank of Ireland, the head office
of which is in London. We have heard a lot, and quite
rightly, aboul the banks in Ireland collecting Irish
money for investment in England. The banks in Ire-
land, we cannot call them Irish banks, require a lot of
reforming. Of course, now that Sir T. Esmonde has
become a ‘‘ Hungarian ” he will at once resign from the
Board of the so-called ‘“'National ’’ Bank, and start a
campaign against the acceptance of their paper money
in Ireland. It will no doubt be a wrench and involve
a monetary sacrifice on the part of the prodigal ex-
militiaman who has returned to his allegiance.  But
then, ““Cathleen ni IToulihan, your way’s a thorny
way.””  We regret to observe that some days after Sir
Thomas joined the Green Hungarian Band that he
attended TN T,0NDON in his capacity as director of the
Nation-sucking ‘‘ National ” Bank. Is Irish compul-
sory in the oxaminations for clerkships of this bank
governed in London, of which our latest ‘‘ Hungarian’
is a dircctor ?

Wo see that Mr. Dolan, the Empee who has not yet
resigned, Dhas started a ““ No. 1, Vol. I.,”” of his own!
Certainly if new papers could save a nation Ireland
would be saved indeed. Mr. Dolan’s paper is called
The Leitrim Guardian. Tt is the local organ of the

imported ‘‘ Hungarian’ policy; it is an additional
tin whistle in the Green Hungarian Band. Here is a
_specimen of raimeis from the leading article in No. 1,
Vol. I.: —“"To build up Ireland means to damage Eng-
land.  An increase of Irish trade means a decrease of
English trade, the establishment of Trish industries
means the ruin of corresponding English industries,
and any wealth that is kept in Ireland is withheld
from England.”” This twaddle need only be put before
any level-headed man, and it would be superfluous to
comment on it. As an example of how the verb ‘‘ to
do’’ differs from the verb ‘“to say,”’ even in ‘ Hun-
garian ”’ circles, we note that Mr. Dolan’s paper states
editorially that “ we are determined not to publish any
advertisements of English manufacturers.” This is a
definite statement on page three. On page one we see
an advertisement of six makes of British cycles, and not
even a mention of such a thing as a cycle made or put
together in Treland. On page two we sco advertise-
ments from two boot and shoe retail shops, and, in the
absence of information to the contrary, we presume
that these shops sell boots other than Irish; indeed, we
arc not informed that they stock any Irish boots.
Where does the ‘‘ boycott ' of English goods come in
here? On page two also there is an advt. of Irish
ales and stout, but ales and stout are excisable articles
and are a source of revenue to the ““ nefarious’’ empire
that overtaxes this country. Is it partof ‘‘Hungarian’’
tactics to advertise excisable articles in No. 1, Vol. 1.,
amongst Leitrim  Flungarians’”? What has the Anti-
Revenue League to say to this? And in a contributed
article on the first page of ‘“No. 1, Vol. I.,”” we are
told : —*“ If they (the M.P.’s) withdraw from the Eng-
lish Parliament and meet in the capital of Treland—
they will then be the real representatives of the people
-—the real members of Parliament of Ireland, instead
of sitting at your expense in a foreign Parliament. If
they do this they can get the people all over Ireland to
boycott English Manufactures—and the result would
be that we would-have new Irish Industries springing
up that would give employemnt to our people.”” This
gentleman would have us believe that the M.P.’s, who
are said to be useless on the ‘‘ floor,”” would hecome magi-
cians and conjurers if they left it! Perhaps they could
even prevent Mr. Dolan’s paper from advertising six
makes of British bicycles, with never a mention in the
ad. of an Trish bicycle ?

We take the following advertisement from Mr. Wm.
Murphy’s ‘“ Nationalist organ,”” the Independent, of
July 25th: —“ Wanted at once for country two Hebrew
Tailors, single, or one man with working family ; plenty
work ; good wages to right party. Address 5462, In-
dependent Office.”’

We fear Sinn Fein will run to seed, or to empty talk,
which is much the same thing. Tt is almost six years
ago since we took a stand at the Dublin Ilorse Show
and put Sinn Fein on the signboard. Since then the
Martynettes have dragged the motto in the intellectual
gutter, and there is probably more raimeis talked now
about Sinn Fein than was ever talked about 'the politi-
cian’s ‘“ no far distant date.”” The ‘‘ Green Hungarian
Band,’’ otherwise the Martynettes, are doing their best.
to make the healthy cry Sinn Fein a laughing stock.

We could make a column of ‘‘ Flumour of the Week "’
out of the speeches and comments on the Enniscorthy
mecting.

In the Sinn Fein policy boycotting Linglish manufac-

The writer warms to it:—‘ No English plough, or
harrow, or reaper, or binder must be allowed on Trish
land.” We certainly hope that the day is ‘‘ not far
distant ’’  when Irish-made ploughs, etc., for Irish

we only recently refused to quote for, though requested
to do so! We are only in the farthing place—in the
tall talking line! We preach—*‘‘ Support Irish manu-
facture ”’ on its merits—the Enniscorthy Echo bellows
 boycott English manufacture.”” The Zcho takes an
advertisement of foreign starch which we were asked to
quote for and refused! There’s a picture for you!
Who says that raimeis is dead? If you want to buy an
English soap you will find a pictorial advertisement of
a brand of English soap tlat we would not advertise if
they paid us a sovereign or ten sovereigns an inch—you
will find it in the Enniscorthy Echo. They preach boy-
cott British goods and they make a revenue out of a
British soap advertisement, British bicycles, British
pills, and British starch! ~ But one need not be sur-
prised at anything a ‘‘ Martynette ’’ paper says, now
that the Martynette ‘‘ revolutionists’’ have taken an
ex-militiaman to their bosom!

We read in the Zcho:—“Let who will speak for
British manufacture, the foundry men should speak for
thomselves.”” And the Zeckho will continue to advertise
British pills, soap, bicycles, etc. ~ We would suggest
that the chemists of Enniscorthy should form a deputa-
tion that might be called the *“ Sinn Fein Enniscorthy

Emniscorthy). Even though you may, as per advertise-
ment, buy British starch rather than Silverspring starch
made in Cork, you cannot buy a Dennehy’s hat; if you

?re a true Martynette you must buy a hat made in Wex-
ord !

We read in the Znniscorthy Echo:—* No young fel-
low will keep company with any young lady who gave
her money to support the foreigner. And patriotic
young women will insist that young men must cover
their heads with home manufacture.”” But supposing
the young lady has ironed her collar with British-made
starch! ~ And supposing on being accused of the
enormity she retorts that she saw the ad. in the Ennis-
corthy Echo! What then? Oh, ““ Cathleen ni Houli-
han, your way’s a thorny way.”” To be a J.P., or not

_to be a J.P.,; Mr. Donohoe!

Was the Enniscorthy meeting a success? We cannot
offer a first hand view, as we were not there. Our con-
temporary the Wexford Free Press places the following
caption to its report of the proceedings:—‘*Sinn
Fiddlesticks! New Factionists Meet at Enniscorthy.
Puzzle : —Find the Policy. Small and Non-Representa-
tive Gathering.  Dublin Corporators Instruct North
Wexford.””

Our conﬁémporary says:—

On Sunday a public meeting was held in tho Abbey Square,
Enniscorthy, for the purpose of explaining the Sinn Fein “ Policy.’
“The day was a beautifully fine onc, well suited for the hold'ng of
an open-air mecting. In spite of the fact that the meeling was
announced by poster all over, the county, and though cxcursion
trains at unprecedently low rates were run from all parts, the
aftendance was anything at all but what was oxpected, and the
rion-attendance of public men—with one or two exceplions—was
convincing proof that in tho County Wexford, at lea:t, the men
in whom tho eclectors repose their trust are not d'sposed to lead
their constituents into disruption and disunion. . It may well be
said that of the attendance at Sunday’s meoting, fully three-foutths
attended as a matter of mere curiosity, and of the other fourth at
least one-half were altracted by the crowd. On the arrival of the

We quote further from the Wexford Free Press:—

Thore was a lack of interest, not to mention enthusiasm, about
tho wholo proccodings. It had been stated that fourtcen bands
had promised to attend the demonstration, but not one turned up
at tho meoting. Decorations, too, wero to have been carried out,
but so far as ono could soo, theso consisted of two flags from the
“National Council” rooms. The platform was crected in the

And again: —

Sinn Fein Magistrato Donohoe wrote: “If he were at home he
should be very pleased to attend the meeting.” Several voices
askod “ wasn’t Donohoe here?’”  Of course, there was no answer,
but all the same ho was there.  Yes, and right on the outskirts of
the erowd.”

The leading article in the ‘‘ Free Press’’ is quite a
slashing attack on the ‘‘ Iungarians.” Tt is a pity
that these ' Hungarians’’ who have imported their
ideas have got themselves mixed up with the healthy cry,
Sinn Fein, for they injure a good cause, and they create
a violent reaction in many quarters in favour of a Par-
lianentary Party which badly needs reorganisation and
repairs. Our contemporary, tke “ Free Press,’’ says:—

The promoters of last Sunday’s meecting at Enniscorthy have
unwittingly rendered a good service to the National cause in
. North Wexford. They have exhibited the Sinn Fein faction in
its nakedness, and the people of the whole country have seen that
it is something to be despised, distrusted and avoided. The
utmost efforts were made by the persons who have been hatching
this plot against National unity to secure the attendance of any-
body and everybody at the meeting.  Special appeals were made
to all those who might be suspected of any hostility to the Irish
Parliamentary Party, and although these advances did not result
successfully, unless in a few cases, the Sinn Fein managers scem
to have had a pretty complete list of all the cranks and sorcheads
in the county. There was loud talk of a mighty and representa-
tive gathering that was to be, and of numerous bands—the exact
number was, we believe, fourtcen.  This is what we were pro-
mised, but when it came to the day and the hour of the meeting,
there was no great asiembly, whether of representative men or
anyone clse; there were no bands; there was no enthusiasm; and,
as our readers will see for themselves, there was no policy to
propound.

Later on our contemporary refers to the ‘‘fiery
Cole,”’ and to the ex-militia man who now is member
of the ‘“ National Council,”’ as ‘“ the Gold Ornament of
the Irish Party.”

We further read in the leading article in the ‘‘ Free
Press’’ :—

The Sinn Fein Bank Director and the Sinn Fein J.P. ought to
walk through the streets of Enniscorthy arm in arm, with a small
boys’ band in front, as a pair of the most wonderful creatious of
Irish political eccentricity found since the beginning of this
century.

And again :—

Sir Thomasis Chairman of the County Councils’ General Council,
and no doubt already sees himself Prime Minister in the imaginary
Cabinot which the Sinn Feiners are so fond of talking about.

Mr. Edward Martyn may have to fight for his position
now that the aristocratic Esmonde has burned Lis
militia epaulets, and has become a member of the
“National Council.””  Truly has it been said—‘‘ Un-
happy is the head that wears a crown.” Edward VIII.
is none too secure as King of the ‘‘ Hungarians,” we
should say.

out their humorous sides. Tke ‘fiery Cole,”’ as the

that the second characteristic of the ‘‘ policy *’, was that
<'it recognised that a nation was made up, of units.
Well, now, what a discovery! One of the,things.to be
done was ‘“ to do nothing that would help foreign indus-
trialism by selling foreign goods in this country.” '~ We
wonder have foreign vegetables ‘a special dispensation !
The ¢ fiery one *’ declared :—‘‘ Let no Irishman join the
R.I.C.; and. let no woman give her arm to the member
of any of the British armed forces.”” What about ex-
militiamen ‘and existing J.P.’s? The ‘‘fiery one’
then indulged in a regular feast of ‘‘lets,”” though he
forgot to wind up with ‘“let us all go to heaven in a
cockle shelk’’ ¥

Alderman Kelly, who forgot to pull down any Union
Jacks during the King’s visit, considered ‘‘ the man
who would not pay one shilling extra for a pair of boots
made in Ireland as against a pair of boots made in Eng-
land as acting criminally towards his country.”  Sup-
posing, if only for a change, that Alderman Kelly said
that the Irish workingmen who refused to work in Ire-
land on the production of Irish goods for a shilling or
a few skillings per week less than'standard rate of wages
so as to help Irish manufacturers to meet competition,
were acting criminally towards their country! Catch
Alderman Kelly saying anything of that sort! The
Trades Hall, in Capel Street, where the drinking bar
is, might bark at the Alderman, and he might find
voters shy at the next elections.

Mr. Arthur Griffith, a gentleman who recently re-
covered some money by tke aid of British law in Ireland,
said that ‘“ Parnell was one of the greatest leaders that
Ireland ever produced.” = We thoroughly agree, but
might we ask why did Sir.T. Esmonde not stick to him?
The Conductor of the Green Hungarian Band declared—
*“ They were asked what they intended to do. The first
step they intended to take was get the Irish members
from the House of Commons.”” Mr. Dolan, M.P. (not
yeb resigned), referred to tle amount of money ‘‘that was
being paid out annually to insurance companies in Eng-
land, and deplored the fact that the people had not
sufficient enterprise to start insurance companies of ther
own.” What is to prevent the people starting an in-
surance company, or two or three of them? But then,
according to the Conductor of the ‘‘ Green Hungarian
Band,”” the first step is to get ‘“’the Irish members from
the House of Commons."’

Here you have the whole game of ‘“ Hungarian ’’ hum-
bug in a nutshell. There are few people can have a less
flattering opinion of the Parliamentarians than we have;
so little do we think of them that we go on as best we
can stimulating the verb ‘‘ to do,”” not bothering over-
much about the M.P.’s; if, Lappily, the M.P.’s can be

Already we have the General Council of County Coun-
cils, and that body, if it liked, could invite the Chair-
men of, or delegates from,'obher representative bodies
other than County Councils, and form forthwith a
Council of two or three hum}rcd, or a thol}sgnd, for tke
matter of that; what need is there of waiting to get
the Irish members from the Touse of Commons?’ The
¢ Hungarian "’ business is only a bogey, and so the
attack on the M.P.’s is put up as the first thing to be
done.

The verb ‘“ to do’’ is at everyone’s hand, and at the
hands of every organised body in Ireland if they have
“ga’’ and initiative. The ‘ Hungarians ’ are full of
the verb ‘‘ to say,’”’ and it is convenient for the.m to put
tke tripping up of the M.P.’s as the first work'to their
hand. That gives the players of the ‘‘ Hungarian”
game of humbug something to play with and plenty to
orate about.

The ¢ Hungarian ’’ party won’t do. The Irish Party
and the United Irish League could conceivably become
in fact the National party in the political world; but
we doubt if they will. Redmond, Dillon, and, above-all,
T. P. O’Connor, are out of touch with the times; if all
three resigned tkere might be a chance of bringing the
Party and the U.L.L. into touch with Irish Ireland. =If
the Party—as we fear will be the case—fails to do some-
thing to vecover itself from the kick it got from the Con-
vention, some other moderate and business-like party or
organisation must step into the breach, for the ‘‘ Hun-
garians,” with- their Alderman Kellys and ‘‘fiery

“JTHE country round Dripsey, though it may not be

[ known very well to the tourist, is a rival in the

saying a greab deal. Why the local light railway com-
pany that .opens up this part of the country to traffic
does not run excwisions o Dripsoy and its neighbour-
hood as excursions are run to Blarney, is one of those
things which we cannot fully understand. Perhaps tha
railway directors would consider it; we are sure that
with so many of them directing a little bit of a light
railway that thoy could at least afford the time.

The mill at Dripsey was once a corn mill.  Some
bwenty years ago it commenced its history as a woollen
mill in a moderate way, but fortune did not smile, or
anyway continue to smile, on it, and about five years
ago it was in the hands of one of our banks. It was &
fine mill with potential possibilities, and the modern
building erected ahout twenty years ago probably cost
ahout £8,000 to erect. There is a fresh look about th
sharp outlines of Dripsey mill; it is no antediluvi

it~ was vrobably something like slow speed astern.

Since Mr. O’Shaughnessy bought it, Dripsey, in its

own way, has made Irish industrial history. Dripsey is

now a household word in Irish Revival circles, not only

in Ireland but all over the world where there are exiles

interested in the industrial revival of the old country.
Bt Why was Drivsey in the bank’s hands a few years ago?
‘Why is it going ahead since Mr. O’Shaughnessy took
it over? There are plenty of openings for successful
work in Ireland ; what is really lacking are the people to
take advantage of them'with the right enterprising
grit—people to play the industrial game with spirit.
It is not so much industries for Ireland as Irish people
for industries that is the need of the situation.

We started out seven years ago with that conviction
and it has grown on us and has intensified since. It is not
i futile talky-talky advising underpaid labourers and
needy clerks and half-time artisans to pay extra for
things because they are Irish that will drive Irish in-
dustry ahead—very much the other way. There arc
plenty of openings for successful work in Ireland if
f people will put their backs and their brains into them,
* and if they don’t there is no use blaming the Govern-

ment-—rotten and robbing though it is—or the people
or anyone else but ourselves. ~One man dressed in
British shoddy haranguing another man in Northamptoa
boots on the duty of making sacrifices for Irish industry,
whilst the industrial managers are ‘‘above’’ their
business and the proprietors are at a peoinin garden
party, is only a sight to make our competitors laugh and
move Ireland to tears. Mr. O’Shaughnessy, the
proprietor of Dripsey mills, bears a striking facial re-
semblance to Sir Horace Plunkett. Ile can talk a bit
il of Irish, and, needless to say, he is a thorough-going
& Irish Irelander. He is a keen, calculating and enter-
3 prising man, with whom it is indeed a pleasure to dis-
4] cuss the affairs of Ireland. . He is fortunate in the
manager of the Dripsey mills, Mr. William Cronin.
Perhaps fortunate is not the correct word, for we take
it that one of the chief functions of competent men is
to select the proper lieutenants. If Mr. O’Shaugh-
nessy were not an able man, it is probable that he
would have selected a botch for his manager.

Every place you read of you form a mind-picture of
it, however vague it may be. We had always pictured
Dripsey lifting its chimney-shaft over a flat open
country. Dripsey, as a matter of fact, nestles on one
side of a valley that even in Ireland it would be hard
to beat for beauty. It is not, unfortunately, a very
populated district. The village of Lower Dripsey is,
indeed, a deserted village. Sixty or seventy years ago
_bhere was a great. paper-making mills there, employing
about 400 people. It was hit by some British legisla-
tion concerning the paper industry, and whether that
was the sole cause or not, the industry faded away, and
_the workers, or many of them, .emigrated to Glasgow.
The ruins of the great mill still stand, and one side of
‘the road as you pass along tells part of the story in
terms of roofiess cottages. Away on the other side, the

Dripsey struck us as a very well appointed mill. On
one of the many looms we noticed the date 1907, on
another 1906, and a great automatic spinning mul:
that works in the mill is, we understand, the most
modern in the market. Dripsey makes all its Dress
yarn, and this, of course, adds to the proportion of Irish
labour employed at Dripsey. One of the Douglas
mills, for instance, makes no yarn, they import it all.
Close beside the mills a busy staff of masons are engaged
in building some new houses, to which large gar-
dens will be attached, for workers. Also a new re-
sidence for the manager is in course of erection on a
most picturesque spot commanding a beautiful view of
the valley and of the historic Dripsey Castle. ~We
witnessed all the processes of manufacture from the
wool as it is received into the factory to the finished
cloth. All the wool used is Irish and most of it is pro-
cured from farmers in the locality. The various pro-
cesses of cloth-making from washing the wool to finish-
ing the cloth are very interesting to inspect, but a de-

best, we fear, very unintelligible reading to the non-

mind of Ireland in the Irish Industrial direction; and
work along this line has a wide field for development.
‘We saw wool being washed, dried, dyed, mixed, carded,
woven and so forth, and we feel sure that any of our
readers who may take a trip out to the beautiful valley
where the hum of Dripsey mills resound will be wel-
comed by Mr. Cronin and shown over the place with
the heartiest pleasure. The whole place is worked by
water power, even the fine installation of electric light
is generated from the stream.

The Dripsey mills as they work to-day are an object
lesson in Trish pluck and enterprise. Mr. O’Shaugh-
nessy, who is a big figure.in the Irish Creamery In-
dustry, displayed characteristic pluck in taking over
these mills and turning on the engines full speed—
ahead. e spent a great deal of money on improve-
ments and new machinery, and he has the satisfaction
of knowing that the number of hands and the output
have doubled within the past year and a half. They
had, at the time of our visit, sold as much as the mill
can produce in the next four months. Indeed, with
Dripsey, as with Ireland in general, as we are fond of
emphasising, what is needed is greater production.
Last year Dripsey mills opened accounts in London.
This year they have tapped the Glasgow trade. They
are consulting Glasgow taste and making what that
market particularly desires. In due time when Dripsey
mills are double or four times their present consider-
able size, patriotic Glasgownians may be passing reso-
lutions and making speeches against Dripsey Dumps!

T. Lyons & Co.’s buyer cleared out the entire stock of
“ Kathleens ”’ when he visited in the height of: the
season. Messrs. Cash & Co. have a constant display in
their windows of the very newest Dripsey productions.

The Dublin firms are equally appreciative. We saw
portion.of an order for thousands of yards of a special
flannel for Messrs. Clery & Co. being finished. This,
we understand, is for a Dublin institution.

The Dripsey cloths are used largely by all the leading
capmakers. Messrs. O’Gorman, Dennehy’s, Slattery,
Dublin Hat Co., etc., and most of the makers stamp the
caps ‘‘ The Dripsey Cap.™

There is not a foreigner working in Dripsey
mills, tke manageris an Irishman and an Irish-Ire-
lander, and yet, though it has doubled its hands and
output in about eighteen months, the problem is—how
to keep on increasing the output so as to keep level with
the trade. 3

The water power, by which the mill is solely worked,
electric light and all, is limited, and the doubling of
‘the mill’s capacity for production would necessitate the
introduction of new power—either steam or gas. Drip-
sey in its happy valley has caught the imagination and
the market, and the bold though calculating enterprise
of Mr. O’Shaughnessy may be trusted to keep the
Dripsey flag bravely flying in the woollen market, even
though cloth-making is only a side line to this prominent
figure in the Irish Creamery world.

Shortly after we had left part of Ireland at work,
and the busy hum of Dripsey Woollen Mills, behind us,
we came on a striking but unfortunately characteristic

contrast. We saw a policeman, presumably ‘‘ on duty’’
in this ‘¢ wicked,”” ‘‘disturbed,”” and ‘‘lawless”’
country. And how was this desperate upholder of

order and the rights of property earning his wages on
this sweltering July day? The flippant reader may
suggest—creasing his pole, polishing his boots, or play-
ing a concertina. No. Catching a British burglar
who came over with the Exhibition, or one whose an-
cestors came over with Cromwell, following up a clue to
the lifting of the Dublin Castle trinkets? Wrong
again. The policeman’s hat, coat and waistcoat lay
carefully on the bank of a limpid stream, his trousers
were tucked up to his knees, and the well-paid repre-
sentative of the Law was sitting on a rock and paying
the purling brook the honour of allowing it to lave the
shins of the majesty of the Law! We shook our fingers
at the Law as we passed, and shouted, ‘‘ Ha ha, my
boyo, we’ll show you up.”” But never heeding, probably
not hearing, the Law kept on cooling its shins. The
busy police cost the country an enormous sum, and yet
here is a picture snapped from within almost a stone-
throw of Dripsey’s busy and nation-killing mills. ~ The
people at work ; and the Law—paddling.

Now I am not mentioning these facts as any argu-
~ ment for granting a Catholic University. I have given

to people’s minds. It is time to bring up these facts

a little reality into the discussion of University matters

ould be several squeezed to death in the porch if they

wded in to-morrow. The place could not hold them.
Now, what I urge is, that we must change our way

modical matters. There are still people—the

unjustified contempt, the unmerited neglect of their
follow-Catholics. It is high time that we gave up this
unscrupulous political cruelty, and that, in the light of

N my last article T pointed out within the limits of
time at my disposal, and of your valuable space,
some of the errors and inconsistencies of ‘‘ Sacerdos,
D.D.”” May I be permitted to point out some others
before coming to deal with Sagapc. What every care-
ful reader of this controversy must have observed is—
that ““ Sacerdos, D.D.,”” and others like him are en-
gaged in hypothetical cases—theorising, putting up
targets as if for amusement and firing at them; and
evading altogether the practical question. The Catho-
lic Hierarchy of Ireland, dealing with the concrete
managerial question of primary schools in Ireland, and
speaking as the guardians of the religious interests of
the flocks committed to their charge, have made the
specific statement—‘‘ That any limitation or restriction
of the control which is now exercised by managers over
the schools of the National system of education would
be so injurious to the religious interests of our people
as to make it imperative on us to resist the introduction
of such a measure, and, in case it were adopted, to re-
consider our whole position in relation to these
schools.” To my mind that statement settles the whole
question for us Irish Catholics. On this I have been
insisting all through: and so has ‘“ Cormac,” who is
neither a cleric nor a manager of a school, but, as his
articles chow, an intelligent, well-educated layman with
much practical experience of life—and not one given
to building castles in the air. The situation as it exists
is accepted by the Catholics of Ireland.  Neither
amongst the clergy, nor the laity, amongst the parents
of the-children, or the representative bodies through
the country is there any indication of dissatisfaction
or revolt. That there are some anti-clericals in Ire-
land few will deny, and ‘‘ Sacerdos, D.D.,”” seems to
admit. I do not think they are numerous. But such
persons are generally noisy. Will ‘“ Sacerdos, D.D.,”
deny that an attitude of hostility to this pronounce-
ment of the Bishops, and above all an active propa-
ganda to do what the Bishops say they would feel ““it
imperative on them to resist,”’ is anything else than an
un-Catholic and anti-clerical attitude? All who take
up such an attitude may not be animated by a rebellious
anti-clerical spirit. That is not the question. I am
looking at the thing from an objective point of view.
I am afraid that there are some who are both subjec-
tively and objectively anti-clerical. Furthermore, the
Bishops, in, their declaration, spoke as the guardians of
the religious interests of the Nation. Were they not~
within their right? Were they not discharging a
solemn duty of their office? They raised no speculative
questions; they dealt not with theories. Neither have
